remediation_cmd: Restrict the security group ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to known source ranges: aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --group-id <SG_ID> --protocol tcp --port <PORT> --cidr 0.0.0.0/0  (then re-add a scoped CIDR)
risk: The scanner has confirmed a workload is genuinely reachable from the internet on a sensitive port by correlating every network layer that must line up: a public IPv4/IPv6 address on the ENI, an internet-gateway route, a permissive security group, and a stateless NACL that allows both the request and the reply. Unlike a single misconfigured SG rule, this means an attacker on the open internet can actually establish a connection to the exposed service (SSH, RDP, a database, or a management port) right now. Automated internet scanners locate and probe such services within minutes, enabling brute-force, exploitation of the listening service, or use as an initial-access foothold.
impact: A live, end-to-end internet path to a sensitive service is an immediate initial-access opportunity that can lead to workload compromise, data theft, and pivoting deeper into the VPC and account.
steps: Identify the exposed resource and effective open port from the finding, then map its ENI, public IP, and security groups: aws ec2 describe-instances --instance-ids <INSTANCE_ID> --query "Reservations[].Instances[].{Eni:NetworkInterfaces[].NetworkInterfaceId,SGs:SecurityGroups,Pub:PublicIpAddress}"
Contain fastest via the security-group path: aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --group-id <SG_ID> --protocol tcp --port <PORT> --cidr 0.0.0.0/0 (and the ::/0 rule if present)

Re-add access only from trusted CIDRs, or eliminate the public entry point by moving the instance to a private subnet and releasing its public IP / EIP: aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress --group-id <SG_ID> --protocol tcp --port <PORT> --cidr <TRUSTED_CIDR>
Where remote administration is required, replace direct exposure with SSM Session Manager (no inbound ports) or an audited bastion host

Verify the path is closed by re-running the exposure scan or checking the SG: aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids <SG_ID> --query "SecurityGroups[].IpPermissions" (VPC Reachability Analyzer via aws ec2 create-network-insights-path can also confirm no path)
Prevent recurrence by enforcing a Firewall Manager security-group policy that blocks 0.0.0.0/0 on sensitive ports and requiring new workloads to launch in private subnets behind a load balancer

remediation_cmd: Patch the affected package to fixedInVersion, e.g. run the patch baseline: aws ssm send-command --document-name AWS-RunPatchBaseline --targets Key=InstanceIds,Values=<INSTANCE_ID> --parameters Operation=Install, then re-scan in Inspector.
risk: Amazon Inspector v2 flagged an ACTIVE, HIGH/CRITICAL package-level CVE in software installed on this EC2 instance's OS. An attacker who reaches the host (or an existing foothold on it) can exploit the vulnerable package for remote code execution, privilege escalation, or denial of service; when the finding is tagged 'exploitable' it means the CVE is on KEV, has a public exploit, or a high EPSS score, so weaponization is likely. Unpatched OS/library CVEs are the single most common initial-access and lateral-movement vector in cloud breaches.
impact: One exploitable host CVE can hand an attacker code execution on the instance, its instance-profile credentials, and a pivot deeper into the VPC.
steps: Confirm the finding and its fixed version: aws inspector2 list-findings --filter-criteria '{"resourceId":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"<INSTANCE_ID>"}]}' --query 'findings[].{cve:vulnerabilityId,pkg:packageVulnerabilityDetails.vulnerablePackages,fix:remediation.recommendation.text}'
Verify the host is SSM-managed so it can be patched: aws ssm describe-instance-information --filters Key=InstanceIds,Values=<INSTANCE_ID>
Apply the vendor fix via the patch baseline: aws ssm send-command --document-name AWS-RunPatchBaseline --targets Key=InstanceIds,Values=<INSTANCE_ID> --parameters Operation=Install
Bake the patched state into a new golden AMI and roll it out so replacement/autoscaled hosts are not reintroduced vulnerable

Re-scan and confirm closure: aws inspector2 list-findings --filter-criteria '{"resourceId":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"<INSTANCE_ID>"}],"findingStatus":[{"comparison":"EQUALS","value":"ACTIVE"}]}'
Prevent recurrence: add AWS Config rule ec2-managedinstance-patch-compliance-status-check and enforce a Patch Manager patch policy / maintenance window across the org so hosts auto-patch on schedule

remediation_cmd: Update password policy: aws iam update-account-password-policy --minimum-password-length 14 --require-symbols --require-numbers --require-uppercase-characters --max-password-age 90 --password-reuse-prevention 24
risk: This check flags an account password policy that is weaker than CIS 1.8 or absent entirely: minimum length under 14, missing symbol/number/uppercase requirements, maximum age over 90 days, or password-reuse prevention under 24. Weak policies let users choose short, simple, or recycled passwords that fall quickly to dictionary, brute-force, and credential-stuffing attacks, and a long max-age keeps a compromised password valid for months.
impact: Elevated likelihood of console credential compromise across all IAM users, plus a direct compliance failure against CIS, PCI-DSS, and NIST password controls.
steps: Review the current policy: aws iam get-account-password-policy (a NoSuchEntity error means no policy is set).

Apply a compliant policy: aws iam update-account-password-policy --minimum-password-length 14 --require-symbols --require-numbers --require-uppercase-characters --require-lowercase-characters --max-password-age 90 --password-reuse-prevention 24.
Verify: re-run aws iam get-account-password-policy and confirm each field meets the baseline.
Notify users that non-compliant passwords must be rotated at next sign-in, since the new rules apply on the next password change.
Prevent recurrence: enforce the AWS Config managed rule IAM_PASSWORD_POLICY and consider centralizing authentication in IAM Identity Center.

remediation_cmd: Strip all rules from each default SG so it denies all traffic (CIS 5.4): aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --group-id <SG_ID> --ip-permissions ... and aws ec2 revoke-security-group-egress --group-id <SG_ID> --ip-permissions ... ; migrate workloads to purpose-built SGs
risk: A default security group still has inbound and/or outbound rules, violating CIS 5.4, which requires the default SG to restrict all traffic. Any ENI or instance launched without an explicitly assigned security group silently inherits the default SG, so leftover permissive rules grant network access to resources whose owners never intended to expose them. Because the default SG is easy to forget and applies implicitly, it becomes an invisible allow-path that quietly widens the attack surface.
impact: Resources accidentally attached to a permissive default security group gain unintended network reachability, creating unmanaged exposure and a CIS benchmark failure.
steps: Inspect the default SG's remaining rules: aws ec2 describe-security-groups --filters Name=group-name,Values=default --query "SecurityGroups[].{Id:GroupId,Vpc:VpcId,In:IpPermissions,Out:IpPermissionsEgress}"
Remove all inbound rules: aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --group-id <SG_ID> --ip-permissions '<COPIED_INBOUND_JSON>' (or revoke each rule individually)
Remove the default allow-all egress rule: aws ec2 revoke-security-group-egress --group-id <SG_ID> --ip-permissions 'IpProtocol=-1,IpRanges=[{CidrIp=0.0.0.0/0}]'
Migrate any workload that relied on the default SG to a purpose-built least-privilege SG: aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute --instance-id <INSTANCE_ID> --groups <PURPOSE_BUILT_SG_ID>
Verify the default SG now has zero rules: aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids <SG_ID> --query "SecurityGroups[].[length(IpPermissions),length(IpPermissionsEgress)]"
Prevent recurrence by enabling the auto-remediable AWS Config rule vpc-default-security-group-closed org-wide so any re-added rule is flagged and stripped

remediation_cmd: Enable IAM database authentication so short-lived IAM tokens replace static passwords: aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <DB_ID> --enable-iam-database-authentication --apply-immediately
risk: IAM database authentication is disabled on a MySQL/PostgreSQL/MariaDB/Aurora-family instance, so the database relies solely on static, long-lived DB passwords. Static credentials get hardcoded in app config, committed to repositories, and shared across services; once leaked they grant persistent database access until manually rotated and are hard to audit per-identity. IAM auth instead issues short-lived (15-minute) tokens tied to IAM principals, centralizing authentication and enabling per-role, revocable access. This is a defense-in-depth gap rather than an open door.
impact: A larger blast radius from credential leakage -- a single exposed static DB password grants durable, poorly-audited access to the database.
steps: Confirm which eligible engines lack IAM auth: aws rds describe-db-instances --query "DBInstances[?IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled==`false`].[DBInstanceIdentifier,Engine]"
Enable IAM database authentication: aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <DB_ID> --enable-iam-database-authentication --apply-immediately
Create a DB user mapped to IAM (e.g. in PostgreSQL: CREATE USER app; GRANT rds_iam TO app;) and attach an IAM policy granting rds-db:connect on that db-user resource ARN
Update applications to fetch a token with aws rds generate-db-auth-token --hostname <ENDPOINT> --port <PORT> --username <DBUSER> and connect over TLS
Verify: aws rds describe-db-instances --db-instance-identifier <DB_ID> --query "DBInstances[0].IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled" returns true, then rotate/retire the old static password

remediation_cmd: Attach a bucket policy that denies non-TLS requests (Effect=Deny, Condition Bool aws:SecureTransport=false) and apply it: aws s3api put-bucket-policy --bucket <BUCKET> --policy file://tls-only.json
risk: This check WARNs when a bucket policy does not effectively deny non-TLS access -- either there is no bucket policy at all, or none of the statements denies requests where aws:SecureTransport is false for all principals, all S3 actions, and the object-level resource. Without a TLS-only deny, clients may reach the bucket over plaintext HTTP, exposing object data and, critically, SigV4 request metadata to on-path (man-in-the-middle) attackers who can intercept or tamper with traffic. Enforcing TLS at the policy layer removes reliance on every client choosing HTTPS.
impact: Data and request credentials can traverse the network in cleartext, enabling interception, tampering, or session hijacking, and violating PCI-DSS 4.1 / HIPAA transmission-security requirements.
steps: Confirm scope: aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket <BUCKET> and check for a Deny statement conditioned on Bool aws:SecureTransport=false covering Principal '*', Action 's3:*', and Resource arn:aws:s3:::<BUCKET>/*.

Author a tls-only.json statement: {"Sid":"DenyInsecureTransport","Effect":"Deny","Principal":"*","Action":"s3:*","Resource":["arn:aws:s3:::<BUCKET>","arn:aws:s3:::<BUCKET>/*"],"Condition":{"Bool":{"aws:SecureTransport":"false"}}} -- merge it into the existing policy rather than overwriting other statements.
Apply it: aws s3api put-bucket-policy --bucket <BUCKET> --policy file://tls-only.json
Ensure the Resource list includes BOTH the bucket ARN and the /* object ARN so the deny covers list and object operations (a bucket-only or object-only scope leaves a gap).
Verify: attempt a plaintext request (e.g. curl http://<BUCKET>.s3.amazonaws.com/) and confirm a 403 AccessDenied, while HTTPS still succeeds.
Prevent recurrence: add an AWS Config custom/conformance-pack rule (s3-bucket-ssl-requests-only) so any bucket lacking the TLS-only deny is flagged automatically.
